  • We look forward to hosting more enriching and dynamic sessions and continuing to create spaces for knowledge sharing and collaboration.

Let’s keep the momentum going! Onward and upward!

title: Featured in International Bureau of Fiscal Documentation (IBFD)’s Asia-Pacific Tax Bulletin
summary:
Article on “Global Business, Local Rules: Cracking the Code of e-Invoicing in Malaysia”, providing practical insights into Malaysia’s e-Invoicing framework.
E-Invoicing has emerged as a key element in Malaysia’s tax digitalization agenda, carrying significant implications for multinational enterprises operating in the country. This article examines the regulatory, operational and technological dimensions of implementing an e-Invoicing system within the Malaysian context. It highlights the mandatory compliance requirements set by the Inland Revenue Board of Malaysia, identifies challenges such as cross-border transactions, system integration and alignment with local reporting obligations, and evaluates the risks associated with non-compliance. Strategies for effective adoption are proposed, including phased implementation, the harmonization of processes with Malaysian regulations and structured stakeholder training. Findings suggest that, in Malaysia, e-Invoicing functions not only as a statutory requirement but also as a mechanism to enhance transparency, efficiency and consistency in tax administration. By situating Malaysia’s approach within its regulatory framework, this article contributes to ongoing discourse on the role of digitalization in strengthening national tax compliance.

title: Navigating The 2025 Budget: Implications For Malaysia's Business Landscape
summary:

Together, we’re shaping a future of financial empowerment and growth.

title: How To Claim Income Tax Reliefs For Your Insurance Premiums (YA 2025)
summary: This comprehensive guide clarifies the various tax relief categories for insurance premiums in Malaysia for the Year of Assessment 2025, specifically highlighting the increased cap of RM4,000 for education and medical insurance. It breaks down the qualifying criteria for life, medical, and education policies, explains how to interpret annual insurance statements for tax filing, and provides practical examples on maximizing claims between different relief buckets to optimize a taxpayer's final position.
content: Author: Pugaleshwaran Raja Kumaran, Tax Executive Director at ThinkTX Consultants and a trusted tax partner for RinggitPlus.

title: Digital Currency Taxation in Malaysia
summary: This article examines the taxation of digital assets in Malaysia under the Income Tax Act 1967, clarifying that while cryptocurrencies are not recognized as legal tender, transactions involving them may still attract tax obligations. It details the distinction between revenue assets, which are subject to income tax, and capital assets, which are currently exempt from Capital Gains Tax (CGT), while providing guidance on valuation methods like FIFO and the critical importance of maintaining comprehensive records for audit readiness.
content: Authors: Pugaleshwaran Raja Kumaran, Executive Director, Tax, and Steffi Manisha Arokiam, Associate Director, both at ThinkTx Consultants Sdn Bhd.

title: Estate Taxes: What You Should Know
summary: This article provides a foundational overview of estate taxation, defining the assets that comprise a deceased person's estate and distinguishing between testate and intestate administration. While it clarifies that Malaysia abolished estate tax in 1991, the guide details other critical tax obligations that remain relevant, including Real Property Gains Tax (RPGT) on asset disposals, stamp duty on property transfers, and the income tax responsibilities of an executor during the estate's administration.
content: Author: Pugaleshwaran Raja Kumaran, Executive Director, Tax at ThinkTx Consultants Sdn Bhd.

title: How To Claim Tax Deductions For Your Parents
summary:
How To Claim Tax Deductions For Your Parents

This article explains how Malaysian taxpayers can claim up to RM8,000 in tax relief for their parents' medical expenses, including treatments, specialist visits, and full medical check-ups. It outlines the eligibility criteria, such as residency and medical certification requirements, and provides guidance on the necessary documentation and the process for filing these claims via the MyTax portal.
content: Author: Steffi Manisha Arokiam, Tax Director at ThinkTX Consultants and a trusted tax partner for RinggitPlus.

title: How to File Your Taxes if You Changed or Lost Your Job in 2025 (YA 2025)
summary: This article provides a guide for taxpayers who experienced changes in their employment status during 2025, outlining how to consolidate income from multiple employers and the specific requirements for reporting retrenchment compensation. It details the importance of collecting all necessary EA forms, the tax exemptions available for loss of employment benefits, and the key deadlines for filing through the MyTax portal in 2026.
content:
The documents you provided were authored and published by the following tax professionals and organizations:
  • Latest Tax Updates (January 2026): Prepared by the ThinkTX team, including Thomas Teoh (Senior Advisor), Pugaleshwaran Raja Kumaran (Executive Director, Tax), and Steffi Manisha Arokiam (Tax Director).
  • Budget 2026: Key Highlights: Published by ThinkTX Consultants Sdn Bhd.
  • CCH Articles: These expert insights were published by Wolters Kluwer (CCH) and cover strategic tax topics such as dividend tax, estate taxes, and digital currency taxation.
  • Starting 1 July: Rental and Leasing Services: Co-authored by Pugaleshwaran Raja Kumaran and Steffi Manisha Arokiam for ThinkTX Consultants Sdn Bhd.
  • How to File Your Taxes if You Changed or Lost Your Job in 2025: Written by Pugaleshwaran Raja Kumaran, who serves as a trusted tax partner and content reviewer for RinggitPlus.

title: The new 2% Dividend Tax: Strategic insights and impact on high-value shareholders
summary: This article provides an analysis of the newly introduced 2% dividend tax, exploring its strategic implications for high-value shareholders and its impact on corporate profit distribution planning.

title: Budget 2026: Key Tax Highlights
summary: This document details the extended transitional period for e-Invoicing Phase 4 and outlines significant changes to Sales and Service Tax (SST) policies, including reduced rates for rental services and new exemptions for the construction and manufacturing sectors
content: This comprehensive guide summarizes the major tax reforms from Malaysia's 2026 Budget, covering the introduction of the profit distribution tax, new corporate incentives for AI and automation, the upcoming carbon tax, and expanded personal tax reliefs for families and homeowners.

title: Latest Tax Updates: e-Invoicing and SST (January 2026)
summary: This document provides a summary of key tax changes announced by the Prime Minister on January 5, 2026, focusing on e-Invoicing implementation and Sales and Service Tax (SST) policy adjustments.

title: Malaysia Personal Income Tax Guide 2026 (YA 2025)
summary: This guide serves as an all-in-one resource for taxpayers filing their income tax returns in 2026 for income earned throughout the 2025 calendar year. It covers the essential steps and rules for both first-time and experienced filers: Filing Basics: Explains who is required to file (salaried employees earning roughly RM3,111/month and above, and all business/freelance earners). It notes that for 2026, the MyKad number officially serves as the Tax Identification Number (TIN) for citizens. The MyTax Portal: A step-by-step walkthrough of the LHDN MyTax online system, including how to register, log in, and navigate "EzHasil Services" to access e-Filing. Deadlines & Forms: Form BE (Employment income): Deadline is April 30, 2026 (with a grace period until May 15 for e-Filing). Form B (Business income): Deadline is June 30, 2026 (with a grace period until July 15 for e-Filing). Maximizing Refunds: Details how claiming tax reliefs (like the new housing loan interest relief and expanded medical insurance caps) can lower your "chargeable income" to potentially secure a full refund of the Monthly Tax Deduction (MTD/PCB) already paid. Documentation: Highlights the importance of the EA Form provided by employers and the necessity of keeping receipts for all relief claims for at least seven years in case of an LHDN audit. Special Scenarios: Provides advice for specific situations, such as individuals who changed jobs during the year, those who were retrenched, and foreigners working in Malaysia. Checkout this article on Ringgitplus.com by Steffi Manisha Arokiam

title: Income Tax Relief Malaysia: What’s New For YA 2025?
summary: This article outlines the significant changes and expansions to personal income tax reliefs for the Year of Assessment (YA) 2025 (the taxes you file in 2026). The key updates include: New First-Time Homeowner Relief: A major addition allowing first-time homebuyers to claim tax relief on housing loan interest—up to RM7,000 for properties priced up to RM500,000, and up to RM5,000 for properties priced between RM500,001 and RM750,000. Increased Insurance Caps: The tax relief limit for Education and Medical Insurance premiums has been raised from RM3,000 to RM4,000. Expanded Medical Expenses: The relief for medical expenses (capped at RM10,000 total) now includes a higher sub-limit of RM6,000 for child learning disabilities (Autism, ADHD, etc.). It also covers a wider range of self-testing medical devices like glucometers and blood pressure monitors. Sports & Care for Parents: The RM1,000 sports relief has been expanded to include expenses for parents. Additionally, medical treatment and care relief for parents (up to RM8,000) now explicitly includes biological grandparents. +1 Environmental & Tech Incentives: The RM2,500 relief previously for EV charging equipment has been expanded to include the purchase of household food waste composting machines. Increased Disabled Relief: Fixed relief amounts for disabled individuals, disabled spouses, and those with disabled children have all been increased to further reduce their taxable income. The article emphasizes that these changes are designed to ease the cost of living and encourage homeownership and healthy lifestyle choices. Checkout this article on Ringgitplus.com by Pugaleshwaran Raja Kumaran

title: How To Check If You Need To File Taxes In Malaysia
summary: The article explains the criteria for determining whether an individual is required to submit an income tax return to the Inland Revenue Board (LHDN). It highlights that the necessity to file depends on your total annual income and source of income: Salaried Employees: You must file if your annual employment income exceeds RM37,333 (roughly RM3,111 per month). Even if your employer already deducts Monthly Tax Deduction (MTD/PCB), filing a return is often beneficial to claim tax reliefs and secure a refund for overpaid tax. Business & Freelance Income: There is no minimum threshold; any income earned from independent work, side businesses, or freelancing must technically be declared using Form B. Active Tax Files: If you have previously registered a tax file, you should continue to file a return (even a "zero tax" return) until you inform LHDN of a change in status, such as retirement. Deadlines: For the Year of Assessment 2025, the e-Filing deadline is April 30, 2026 (for employment income) and June 30, 2026 (for business income), with a 15-day grace period for online submissions. The article also advises using the MyTax portal to check your tax identification number (TIN) and manage your filings. Check out the article on Ringgitplus.com by Steffi Manisha Arokiam

title: Understanding capital statements: An overview and importance in recent times
summary:
Key takeaways

​

Challenges of High-Net-Worth Individuals: Individuals with substantial wealth, known as high-net-worth individuals, face unique challenges due to complex financial structures, diverse income streams, and significant assets. The Inland Revenue Board of Malaysia (IRBM) conducts thorough tax investigations for such individuals to ensure compliance and transparency.


Tax Investigation: Tax investigation is a crucial tool for ensuring tax compliance, where the IRBM conducts a thorough examination to detect and prevent tax evasion and fraud. Factors triggering investigations include inconsistencies in tax returns, industry-based investigations, and information from third parties.


Inconsistency of Income with Assets Owned: Inconsistencies in income with owned assets can trigger tax investigations. The IRBM has special powers to access individuals' properties and financial records to assess their assets and income capacity, potentially uncovering tax discrepancies.


Capital Statement: A capital statement provides a detailed overview of an individual's financial position, including assets, liabilities, income, and expenditures. It is essential for high-net-worth individuals to maintain accurate and detailed capital statements to support their tax filings and demonstrate transparency.


Impact of AI in Tax Administration: The IRBM is exploring the use of AI-assisted asset tracking and identifying technology to enhance the process of examining asset declarations and detecting hidden or undeclared wealth. Understanding tax investigation processes is crucial for professionals and citizens to contribute to a transparent and equitable economic system.



Table of contents​
Introduction

Tax Investigation

Capital Statement

Conclusion
content:
Introduction In the field of tax investigation, extraordinary wealth presents unique challenges for an individual. An individual who possess substantial wealth are commonly known as high-net-worth individuals. These individuals often have complex financial structures, diverse income streams and significant assets. This complexity leads to a more thorough scrutinization approach by our tax authority, the Inland Revenue Board of Malaysia (IRBM) rather than a regular tax audit which is a formal examination, to ensure proper compliance and transparency. Extraordinary wealth refers to a level of financial assets, income and investments that significantly exceeds the average within a given population. This wealth may be accumulated from the following forms:
  • Diverse Asset Portfolios
  • Extensive investments in real estate, stocks, bonds, private equity and luxury assets such as yachts and art.
  • Multiple Income Streams
  • Earnings from various sources, including business profits, dividends, interests, royalties and capital gains.
  • Complex Financial Structures
  • Use of trusts, offshore accounts and intricate corporate structures to manage and protect wealth.
  • Significant Expenditures
  • High level of spending on luxury goods, philantrophy and lifestyle maintenance.
Tax Investigation With such complex and intricating routes, one of the crucial tools in ensuring tax compliance is tax investigation. A tax investigation is a thorough examination conducted by the IRBM to ensure that individuals accurately report their income and comply with tax laws and regulations. The primary objective is to detect and prevent tax evasion and fraud. This investigation can be triggered by various factors, including discrepancies in tax returns, random selections, industry-based investigations or information received from third parties. One primary set off is inconsistency of income with assets owned. This includes inconsistencies with lifestyle, spending patterns and unexplainable surprise wealth. The IRBM with its special powers conferred under Section 78, 79, 80 and 81 of the Income Tax Act 1967 allows full access to any individual’s properties and personal records at any point of time and being able to obtain information from businesses and financial institutions that individuals transacted with, regardless of the level of confidentiality. Such accessibility allows the IRBM to assess the individual’s assets and identify the income capacity required to maintain such lifestyle. Such assessment could correspondently uncover discrepancies which then leads to the identification of taxes evaded by an individual. Capital Statement This is where a capital statement emerges in investigating high-net-worth individuals. This approach provides a detailed and comprehensive overview of an individual's financial position over a specific period. It demonstrates a transparent record of assets, liabilities, income and expenditures. This statement typically includes detailed information about capital gains and other transactions that affect the capital aspect of taxation. This meticulous document covers all aspects of financial activities which the IRBM eagerly examines to reveal any underreported or hidden assets that may not be apparent through the individual’s standard tax filing. A capital statement comprises of two (2) forms: CP102 – Statement of personal and private expenses, income and earnings. CP103 – Statement of net assets (year-end balance of assets and liabilities). Note: The usual practice of preparation would be for five (5) years of assessment. The detailed components of the capital statement is as follows: Assets: Includes cash in hand, bank balances, investments, properties, vehicles, jewelleries, and other valuable possessions. Liabilities: Covers property loans, hire purchase loans, credit card debts and other financial obligations. Income: Ranges from employment, director’s fees, businesses, rentals, dividends, interests and other sources of income. Expenditures: Comprises lifestyle, children education, travelling and significant purchases. Therefore, a capital statement allows the IRBM to trace the flow of capital assets, identify the legitimacy of financial activities and recognize any discrepancies that may require further investigation. Hence, maintaining a detailed and accurate capital statement is essential for individuals to support their tax filings and demonstrate transparency in their financial dealings. In the event of a tax investigation, having well-documented capital statements (thorough records of capital transactions, investments and other financial activities) can help streamline the process of providing evidence of compliance, navigate the tax investigation effectively and mitigate potential risks or penalties associated with inaccuracies or inconsistencies in tax reporting. Conclusion Our constantly evolving world with the outreach of Artificial intelligence (AI) into the day-to-day operations and lives of individuals are increasing significantly. Earlier this year, we hear the IRBM exploring implementation of AI-assisted asset tracking and identifying technology to enhance the process of examining assets declaration to promptly probe individuals with undeclared, hidden or camouflaged assets and wealth or potentially those intentionally evading taxes. This brings the IRBM direct access to real-time data. With the already-in-use big data analytics and sophisticated information exchange mechanisms by the IRBM, the move to AI in tax administration will lead to the most transparent level of information being available. On that account, maintaining detailed and accurate financial records and diligence in tax affairs is not only a legal obligation but also a safeguard against potential disputes with the IRBM. As future professionals and informed citizens, understanding these processes empowers us to contribute to a transparent and equitable economic system. This article was originally published on 24 July 2024. The content of this article is intended to provide a general guide to the subject matter. Specialist advice should be sought about your specific circumstances. Pugaleshwaran Rajakumaran is a tax consultant and licensed tax agent who has a wide range of experience in Malaysian taxation. He is a passionate writer who actively writes on the development of Malaysian taxation scene.

title: ThinkTx: Updates to e-Invoicing and Sales and Service Tax (SST)
summary:
Updates to e-Invoicing and Sales and Service Tax (SST)


Below are the key changes announced by our Prime Minister during his New Year message on 5 January 2026.


Additional Transitional Period for e-Invoicing Phase 4 (annual revenue up to RM5 million)


A 12-month transitional period has been granted for taxpayers in Phase 4 (extended from the original 6-month period), beginning from 1 January 2026 till 31 December 2026 (initially ending on 30 June 2026) in order to smoothen the implementation process for MSME businesses.


Important note: e-Invoicing Phase 4 implementation date remains unchanged which is 1 January 2026 and it is important for taxpayers to take note that this additional period granted is an extension of the transitional (relaxation) period and not a deferment of the Phase 4 implementation date.
content:
Consolidated e-Invoice for Construction Materials


Effective 1 January 2026, taxpayers involved in the business of wholesale and retail of construction materials are allowed to issue Consolidated e-Invoices, except for transactions exceeding RM10,000.


Changes to SST Policies

Service tax for rental or leasing services (effective 1 January 2026)

Rate reduced from 8% to 6%. 

Annual sales of tenants with MSME status that is exempted from payment of service tax has been increased from RM1 million to RM1.5 million. 

Newly incorporated MSMEs are granted service tax exemption on rental or leasing services for a period of 1 year from the MSMEs’ registration date. 

Service tax exemption for construction contracts. 

For non-reviewable construction contracts signed before 1 July 2025 that is exempted from 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026 is given an extension for 1 year until 30 June 2027. 

Service tax exemption for construction of places of worship (effective 1 July 2025)

Places of worship such as surau, mosque, temple, church and tokong. 

Sales tax exemption for registered manufacturers (effective 1 January 2026)

Granted on raw materials or inputs used for production of animal feed, fertiliser and pesticides

title: Understanding capital statements: An overview and importance in recent times
content:
Key takeaways

​

Challenges of High-Net-Worth Individuals: Individuals with substantial wealth, known as high-net-worth individuals, face unique challenges due to complex financial structures, diverse income streams, and significant assets. The Inland Revenue Board of Malaysia (IRBM) conducts thorough tax investigations for such individuals to ensure compliance and transparency.


Tax Investigation: Tax investigation is a crucial tool for ensuring tax compliance, where the IRBM conducts a thorough examination to detect and prevent tax evasion and fraud. Factors triggering investigations include inconsistencies in tax returns, industry-based investigations, and information from third parties.


Inconsistency of Income with Assets Owned: Inconsistencies in income with owned assets can trigger tax investigations. The IRBM has special powers to access individuals' properties and financial records to assess their assets and income capacity, potentially uncovering tax discrepancies.


Capital Statement: A capital statement provides a detailed overview of an individual's financial position, including assets, liabilities, income, and expenditures. It is essential for high-net-worth individuals to maintain accurate and detailed capital statements to support their tax filings and demonstrate transparency.


Impact of AI in Tax Administration: The IRBM is exploring the use of AI-assisted asset tracking and identifying technology to enhance the process of examining asset declarations and detecting hidden or undeclared wealth. Understanding tax investigation processes is crucial for professionals and citizens to contribute to a transparent and equitable economic system.


ntroduction


In the field of tax investigation, extraordinary wealth presents unique challenges for an individual. An individual who possess substantial wealth are commonly known as high-net-worth individuals. These individuals often have complex financial structures, diverse income streams and significant assets. This complexity leads to a more thorough scrutinization approach by our tax authority, the Inland Revenue Board of Malaysia (IRBM) rather than a regular tax audit which is a formal examination, to ensure proper compliance and transparency.


Extraordinary wealth refers to a level of financial assets, income and investments that significantly exceeds the average within a given population.


This wealth may be accumulated from the following forms:

Diverse Asset Portfolios

Extensive investments in real estate, stocks, bonds, private equity and luxury assets such as yachts and art.

Multiple Income Streams

Earnings from various sources, including business profits, dividends, interests, royalties and capital gains.

Complex Financial Structures

Use of trusts, offshore accounts and intricate corporate structures to manage and protect wealth.

Significant Expenditures

High level of spending on luxury goods, philantrophy and lifestyle maintenance.


Tax Investigation


With such complex and intricating routes, one of the crucial tools in ensuring tax compliance is tax investigation. A tax investigation is a thorough examination conducted by the IRBM to ensure that individuals accurately report their income and comply with tax laws and regulations. The primary objective is to detect and prevent tax evasion and fraud. This investigation can be triggered by various factors, including discrepancies in tax returns, random selections, industry-based investigations or information received from third parties.


One primary set off is inconsistency of income with assets owned. This includes inconsistencies with lifestyle, spending patterns and unexplainable surprise wealth. The IRBM with its special powers conferred under Section 78, 79, 80 and 81 of the Income Tax Act 1967 allows full access to any individual’s properties and personal records at any point of time and being able to obtain information from businesses and financial institutions that individuals transacted with, regardless of the level of confidentiality. Such accessibility allows the IRBM to assess the individual’s assets and identify the income capacity required to maintain such lifestyle. Such assessment could correspondently uncover discrepancies which then leads to the identification of taxes evaded by an individual.


Capital Statement


This is where a capital statement emerges in investigating high-net-worth individuals. This approach provides a detailed and comprehensive overview of an individual's financial position over a specific period. It demonstrates a transparent record of assets, liabilities, income and expenditures. This statement typically includes detailed information about capital gains and other transactions that affect the capital aspect of taxation. This meticulous document covers all aspects of financial activities which the IRBM eagerly examines to reveal any underreported or hidden assets that may not be apparent through the individual’s standard tax filing.


A capital statement comprises of two (2) forms:


CP102 – Statement of personal and private expenses, income and earnings.

CP103 – Statement of net assets (year-end balance of assets and liabilities).

Note: The usual practice of preparation would be for five (5) years of assessment.


The detailed components of the capital statement is as follows:


Assets: Includes cash in hand, bank balances, investments, properties, vehicles, jewelleries, and other valuable possessions.

Liabilities: Covers property loans, hire purchase loans, credit card debts and other financial obligations.

Income: Ranges from employment, director’s fees, businesses, rentals, dividends, interests and other sources of income.

Expenditures: Comprises lifestyle, children education, travelling and significant purchases.



Therefore, a capital statement allows the IRBM to trace the flow of capital assets, identify the legitimacy of financial activities and recognize any discrepancies that may require further investigation. Hence, maintaining a detailed and accurate capital statement is essential for individuals to support their tax filings and demonstrate transparency in their financial dealings. In the event of a tax investigation, having well-documented capital statements (thorough records of capital transactions, investments and other financial activities) can help streamline the process of providing evidence of compliance, navigate the tax investigation effectively and mitigate potential risks or penalties associated with inaccuracies or inconsistencies in tax reporting.


Conclusion


Our constantly evolving world with the outreach of Artificial intelligence (AI) into the day-to-day operations and lives of individuals are increasing significantly. Earlier this year, we hear the IRBM exploring implementation of AI-assisted asset tracking and identifying technology to enhance the process of examining assets declaration to promptly probe individuals with undeclared, hidden or camouflaged assets and wealth or potentially those intentionally evading taxes. This brings the IRBM direct access to real-time data. With the already-in-use big data analytics and sophisticated information exchange mechanisms by the IRBM, the move to AI in tax administration will lead to the most transparent level of information being available. On that account, maintaining detailed and accurate financial records and diligence in tax affairs is not only a legal obligation but also a safeguard against potential disputes with the IRBM. As future professionals and informed citizens, understanding these processes empowers us to contribute to a transparent and equitable economic system.


This article was originally published on 24 July 2024.


The content of this article is intended to provide a general guide to the subject matter. Specialist advice should be sought about your specific circumstances.


Pugaleshwaran Rajakumaran is a tax consultant and licensed tax agent who has a wide range of experience in Malaysian taxation. He is a passionate writer who actively writes on the development of Malaysian taxation scene.
